title: The Velvet Debt
blurb: Berlin’s underworld burns—glittering, decadent, and ruthless. Clara Voss lost everything once. She won’t do it again. Six weeks. One ledger. A city waiting to be saved—or ruined forever.
comparable: For fans of *The Shadow of the Wind* by Carlos Ruiz Zafón, *The Night Circus* by Erin Morgenstern, *The Lies of Locke Lamora* by Scott Lynch

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Rusty Nail smelled of old schnapps and newer regret. Clara wiped the rim of a champagne flute with a rag that had long since given up pretending to be white. The glass trembled in her grip—not from the weight of the bottle, but from the way Rusty’s eyes tracked her hips as she moved behind the bar. She poured with deliberate slowness, letting the bubbles cling to the rim before she topped it off. The customer—a tipsy journalist from the *Berliner Tageblatt*—slid a five-mark note across the counter. His fingers lingered.

“Keep the change,” he said.

Clara tucked the bill into her garter without looking up. “Thank you, Herr Müller.”

Rusty leaned against the beer taps, arms crossed over his gut. “You’ll lose that coin,” he muttered.

She didn’t slow. “Not if I drink it first.”

A laugh from the corner table. The journalist wasn’t laughing. He was watching her—really watching her—the way men did when they thought the past was a locked door with the key still inside. Clara met his gaze long enough to let him know the lock was solid, then turned to the stage where the band was tuning up.

Lotte Weber’s bow caught the light as she lifted it to her chin. The violin sang a single, shivering note—high, sharp, like a warning. Clara’s shoulders tightened. She knew that sound. Knew the way Lotte’s fingers tapped the fingerboard when she was thinking, not playing. Knew the way her breath hitched before the first solo.

Rusty wiped a glass with a rag that had seen better days. “You know that girl?”

Clara poured another drink. “I know music.”

“She’s playing your song later.”

The journalist flagged her down again. “Another round?”

Clara slid the glass toward him. “Your tab’s already higher than your morals.”

He grinned, undeterred. “Then I’ll need another.”

Lotte’s bow descended. The first chord of her solo rang out, clean and precise—until it didn’t. A wrong note, sharp and strained, like a door slamming in a dream. Clara’s spine went rigid. That wasn’t Lotte’s mistake. That was a code. Three beats off. Three taps in the dark. Her brother’s signal.

Memory hit like a backhand—old pain, fresh and raw. *Three.* The number of bullets. The number of men who’d come to collect. The number of nights she’d spent watching the wrong shadows in alleyways.

The journalist whistled. “Damn. That girl can play.”

Clara’s knuckles whitened around the champagne bottle. She didn’t blink.

Rusty noticed. “You alright?”

She smiled, slow and dangerous. “Never better.”

The wrong note resolved into something jaggedly beautiful—Lotte weaving the error into the melody, salvaging grace from ruin. Clara watched the violinist’s hands, the way they moved like they were translating something secret. The journalist chattered about the stock market. Rusty wiped the same glass for the third time. Through it all, Clara kept her eyes on Lotte.

The solo ended. The audience clapped—polite, indifferent. Clara didn’t move.

Rusty nudged her. “You’re bleeding on the counter.”

She looked down. A thin line of red tracked from her thumbnail to her wrist. She hadn’t even felt it. She wiped her hand on her apron and kept smiling.

The journalist, oblivious, raised his glass. “To the music.”

Clara poured herself a finger of schnapps from the well. She downed it in one swallow. The burn didn’t touch her.

Lotte stepped offstage. Her shoulders were tight, her jaw set. She didn’t look at the bar. Didn’t look at anyone. Just pushed through the crowd toward the exit.

Clara’s fingers twitched. The kid—Lotte’s brother, wasn’t it?—had been in last week with bruises on his knuckles and a mouth full of excuses. She’d slipped a ten-mark note into his palm when no one was looking.

She set the empty glass down.

Rusty blocked her path. “Where you going?”

“Collecting tips.” She sidestepped him, heading for the door.

Müller the journalist called after her, “You’re not coming back, are you?”

Clara didn’t answer.

Outside, the Berlin night swallowed the sound of the club. Traffic hummed along Friedrichstraße. Lotte was already halfway down the block, her coat pulled tight against the autumn chill. Clara followed at a distance, her heels clicking against the cobblestones. Not close enough to spook her. Close enough to protect.

A tram clattered past, drowning out the violinist’s footsteps. Clara lost sight of her. Paused. Listened.

A child’s cry, half-hidden in the alley between the bakery and the pawnshop. Clara turned. The kid—Lotte’s brother—was pressed against the wall, a cigarette dangling from his mouth. His shirt was torn at the shoulder.

Clara stepped into the alley. The stench of rotting fish and damp stone filled her nose.

The boy’s eyes went wide. “Frau Voss.”

She held out a hand. “Come on, then.”

He hesitated, then grabbed her fingers. His palm was sticky with something darker than blood.

Clara didn’t flinch. “You want to tell me what happened?”

The boy shook his head. “Just got in the way.”

She pulled him close, her coat swallowing his thin frame. Behind them, the violin solo from inside the Rusty Nail swelled into the night—a song of mistakes and redemption.

Clara didn’t look back.

Chapter 2

The Haus der Presse glittered like a chandelier cracked open. Gilded scrollwork framed mirrors that reflected nothing but more gold, while champagne flutes cast fractured light across the marble floors. Clara stood at the back, near the service entrance, her gloved fingers curled around a glass she hadn’t tasted. The air smelled of perfume and political promises.

Klaus Meinhardt stood on the stage, his back to the room, his voice smooth as oil. “Ladies and gentlemen, Berlin’s soul is under siege—not by the usual wolves, but by the jackals in our midst.” He paused. The room leaned in. “Cabarets like the Moka Elegance don’t just entertain. They erode. They corrupt. And worst of all—they profit.”

A ripple of laughter. Men in tails clinked glasses. Women in silk adjusted their pearls and nodded like this was holy writ.

Clara’s jaw tightened. She knew that ledger. Its leather cover had been cut from the same hide as the boots her brother wore the night he died.

Meinhardt dropped the ledger onto the grand piano. The piano was silent. The room wasn’t. “In this ledger,” he said, “you’ll find every bribe, every payoff, every dirty deal that has kept this city drowning in its own filth.”

Clara’s breath didn’t hitch. Her pulse did. Her fingers went still.

The piano lid snapped open. Meinhardt’s fingers hovered over the keys. “And tonight, we burn it.”

A spotlight found the piano. The keys gleamed. The ledger lay open like a confession.

Clara’s champagne turned to ice in her hand.

Meinhardt began to play. Not a waltz. Not a fanfare. A dirge. The room fell silent. The notes were slow, deliberate—each one a hammer blow to her ribs.

*This is not the ledger.*

She knew every page. She knew the ink. She knew the way the numbers were aligned, the way the signatures curved—like her brother’s signature had curved when he signed his life away.

This was a fake. A forgery. A trap.

Meinhardt’s voice rose above the music. “This is what happens when we let artists rule our morality!” He slammed a hand onto the lid. The sound cracked through the room like a gunshot.

The audience erupted. Applause. Whistles. One man stood on a chair.

Clara didn’t move. Didn’t clap. Didn’t smile.

Meinhardt turned, eyes scanning the crowd with the hunger of a man who had already won. His gaze passed over her without pause.

She exhaled.

Then Viktor Blau touched her elbow.

“Clara.”

She turned. Viktor’s face was gaunt in the golden light. His bowtie was loose, his collar damp. He looked like a man who had just lost a bet he couldn’t afford to lose.

“You’re not clapping,” he said.

She sipped the champagne. It was vinegar. “I’ve heard this sonata before.”

Viktor’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. “You always did have a poor sense of timing.”

She set the glass down. “Or excellent taste.”

Meinhardt stepped off the stage, surrounded by admirers. The ledger was passed from hand to hand like a sacred relic.

Viktor lowered his voice. “You need to go. Now.”

Clara watched the ledger. The edges were too new. The ink too fresh. It smelled like ink, not age.

“He’s lying,” she said.

Viktor’s fingers twitched. “Of course he’s lying. But the crowd believes him.”

Clara turned to him. “And you? Do you believe him?”

Viktor looked away. “I believe in consequences.”

She almost laughed. “Since when?”

Behind them, the band struck up a foxtrot. Couples waltzed past, their faces flushed with triumph and champagne. No one noticed Clara. No one ever did.

Viktor leaned closer. “They’re coming for you, Clara. Not just the ledger—the whole empire. The Syndicate. The money. The—”

“My venues?” She interrupted. “My liquor licenses? My reputation?”

He swallowed. “Yes.”

She stepped closer, her heel catching the hem of his coat. “You tip off the press baron, you hand him the rope, and now you tell me to run?”

Viktor’s jaw clenched. “I thought it would be quick. Clean.”

“Like my brother’s funeral?”

The words hung between them. Viktor flinched. Clara felt nothing.

A waiter passed with a tray of caviar on blinis. Viktor took one. Ate it. Didn’t taste it.

“What do you want?” he asked.

She watched Meinhardt. He was holding court now, a king in a press-made kingdom.

“I want the truth,” she said. “I want Meinhardt to choke on it.”

“Clara.”

“And I want the real ledger back.”

Viktor’s breath hitched. “That’s impossible.”

She turned to him. Smiled. “Nothing’s impossible. Not when you control the presses.”

Viktor’s face went pale. “Clara, don’t.”

She walked away without another word.

The service corridor was narrow, dimly lit. The air smelled of ozone and old wiring. She passed a janitor pushing a mop, his cap pulled low. He didn’t look up.

At the end of the hall, the emergency exit glowed green. She pushed through.

Outside, the cold Berlin night hit her like a slap. The cobblestones were wet. The streetlights buzzed. A tram rumbled past, its windows glowing yellow.

Clara didn’t wait for it to pass. She stepped off the curb.

A hand grabbed her wrist.

She turned.

Lotte Weber stood in the alley’s mouth, her coat collar turned up, her violin case clutched tight. Her bow was tucked inside. Her eyes were wide.

“You followed me,” Clara said.

Lotte didn’t let go. “You followed *me* last time.”

Clara pulled her hand free. “I saved your brother.”

“You think I don’t know that?” Lotte’s voice was sharp, brittle. “I know everything. I know about the money. I know about the Syndicate. I know about *you*.”

Clara studied her. The violinist’s cheeks were flushed from the concert hall’s heat. Her lips were chapped. She smelled of rosin and sweat.

“Then you know I’m not the enemy,” Clara said.

Lotte’s fingers tightened on the case. “You let my uncle destroy you on stage tonight.”

Clara looked back at the Haus der Presse. The windows glowed. The music thrummed.

“He’s going to destroy more than me,” she said. “He’s going to burn the whole city.”

Lotte stepped closer. The violin case bumped Clara’s hip.

“Then why aren’t you running?” Lotte asked.

Clara met her gaze. Held it. “Because someone has to stop him.”

Silence. Not comfortable. Charged.

Lotte’s breath fogged in the cold. “And that someone is you?”

Clara didn’t answer.

Lotte exhaled. “He’s got the ledger. You know he does.”

Clara did know.

Lotte lowered her voice. “But he doesn’t have the music.”

Clara’s pulse jumped.

Lotte’s fingers tapped the violin case—three short beats, then a pause, then two more. The code again.

Clara’s stomach flipped. “You have it.”

Lotte didn’t nod. Didn’t speak. Just opened the case.

Inside lay a ledger. Smaller. Older. Leather worn at the edges. The pages were filled with handwriting—neat, precise, damning.

Meinhardt’s signature was on the first page.

Clara’s fingers hovered. She didn’t touch it.

“He’s going to kill you for this,” she said.

Lotte’s smile was thin. “He already tried.”

Clara looked up. Straight into Lotte’s eyes.

Something shifted between them. Not trust. Not yet. But recognition.

A shout echoed from the street. Meinhardt’s men, calling Lotte’s name.

Lotte snapped the case shut. “They’re here.”

Clara didn’t hesitate. She grabbed Lotte’s arm and pulled her deeper into the alley, toward the fire escape rusted onto the building’s side.

“Clara!”

She ignored the voice from the street.

Lotte stumbled after her. “Where are we going?”

Clara’s heel struck metal. The fire escape groaned.

“Somewhere Meinhardt won’t look,” Clara said.

The first shot shattered a window above them.

Lotte gasped.

Clara didn’t flinch. “Move.”

Chapter 3

The Rusty Nail smelled of spilled beer and absent soap. Clara counted the money under the bar’s single weak bulb, the cash spread across the chipped Formica like a fan of playing cards. A floorboard creaked behind her. She didn’t look up.

Viktor slid onto a stool at the far end, close enough to speak but not close enough to touch. His cuffs were frayed. His shoes hadn’t seen polish in weeks.

“You’re late,” she said.

He exhaled through his nose, a sound like a tired horse. “You’re counting other people’s money.”

“My money,” Clara corrected. She stacked the notes into neat piles, the edges crisp. “My empire. My problem.”

Viktor leaned forward, elbows on the bar. The wood groaned. “It’s not yours anymore.”

Clara didn’t stop counting. “Says who?”

“Says the courts. Says the syndicates. Says the men in the back room at the Golden Rooster, the ones with the cufflinks that cost more than your apartment.” He tapped a stack. “You’re touching air, Clara.”

She flipped the top bill. A 100-mark note. Fresh off the press. Literally.

“Meinhardt’s presses?” she asked.

Viktor’s fingers twitched. “Don’t go there.”

“Why not?” She stacked another bill. “I built that press. Me and my brother. That mill on Spreeufer. The one with the broken chimney that smoked like a sinner’s breath.”

Viktor’s glass of schnapps sweated onto the bar. “You sold it two years ago.”

Clara set the last bill down. Fifty stacks. Half a million marks. Not enough. Never enough.

“Did I?” she asked.

Viktor’s mouth twisted. “You signed the papers.”

Clara smiled. It wasn’t a kind smile. “I signed what they put in front of me.”

Silence settled between them, thick as cigarette smoke. Outside, a tram rumbled past, its wheels screeching on wet tracks. The neon sign outside buzzed like a dying insect.

Viktor finally spoke, quiet. “I gave him the ledger’s location.”

Clara’s hands stilled. The last bill stayed where it was, half on the stack, half hanging off.

“You,” she said.

Viktor didn’t meet her eyes. “I thought he’d make it quick. Clean. You’d vanish, the city would move on. No more cabarets. No more danger. No more Clara.”

Clara picked up the bill. Rolled it between her fingers. The ink smelled of oil and old promises.

“And now?” she asked.

Viktor’s voice cracked. “Now he’s got everything. The ledger. The music. The proof. The *music*, Clara.”

Clara slid the bill into her pocket. One by one, she gathered the stacks and tucked them into a canvas sack at her feet. The sack was heavy. The room tilted under the weight.

“You gave him Lotte,” she said.

Viktor flinched. “He already knew about her. He was going to use her anyway. I just—”

“Made it easier.” Clara zipped the sack shut. “You made a deal with the devil.”

“I made a deal to *save* you.”

Clara picked up her coat from the hook behind the bar. The lining was torn. The hem frayed. She didn’t bother brushing off the dust.

“You didn’t save me,” she said. “You handed me to him.”

Viktor’s fingers curled into fists. “What are you going to do?”

Clara draped the coat over her arm. The wool was thin. The winter would kill her before she froze.

“I’m going to take it back,” she said.

Viktor stood so fast his stool tipped. He caught it before it hit the floor, but the sound echoed like a gunshot.

“Clara, stop.” He grabbed her wrist. His fingers were cold. “You can’t fight him. Not now. Not with nothing.”

She looked at his hand. Then at his face. His eyes were bloodshot. His hair was thinning. He looked like a man who had already lost.

Clara pulled her arm free. “I have the money.”

“Money doesn’t print lies,” Viktor said. “Money doesn’t frame you. Money doesn’t—”

“Burn my brother’s name?”

Viktor’s breath hitched. Clara turned toward the door.

“Where are you going?” he asked.

Clara adjusted her coat. The lining caught on something. She tugged it free, revealing a long, thin knife tucked into the inner pocket. The blade was dull. The handle was wrapped in black tape.

“To remind Meinhardt,” she said, “that cabarets don’t just entertain. They endure.”

Viktor’s face went gray. “Don’t.”

Clara stepped into the alley behind The Rusty Nail. The brick walls were slick with rain. The fire escape groaned under her weight as she climbed. The sack of money banged against her hip with every step.

At the top, she paused. The city sprawled beneath her, a carpet of neon and shadow. Somewhere out there, Lotte Weber was running. Somewhere, Meinhardt was drinking champagne and counting his victories.

Clara pulled the knife from her coat. The metal was cold. The edge was uneven.

She tested the blade against her thumb. A thin line of red welled up.

She put the knife away.

Footsteps echoed below. Not Viktor’s. Not light enough. Not hesitant enough.

Clara melted into the shadows of the fire escape’s landing. The sack of money hit the metal grate with a soft *thud*.

A man cleared his throat. A match flared. The scent of sulfur cut through the damp air.

“Clara Voss,” a voice said. “I was told you’d be here.”

She didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

The man took a step closer. His shoes squeaked on the wet metal.

“You don’t remember me,” he said. “But I remember you. You hit me with a bottle at the Moka. Broke my nose. Left me bleeding on the dance floor.”

Clara’s fingers curled into fists inside her coat pockets.

The man took another step. The match went out. The darkness swallowed him whole.

“Name’s Bruno,” he said. “Used to work the door at your club. Before Meinhardt bought the lease.”

Clara’s breath fogged in the cold. The sack of money sat between them, unguarded.

Bruno’s shadow shifted. A gun clicked.

Clara exhaled. The match flared again. Bruno’s face was half-lit, half in shadow. His smile was a knife wound.

“He says you’re worth more alive,” Bruno said. “But I don’t like your face.”

The gun leveled at her chest.

Clara didn’t flinch. Didn’t reach for the knife. Didn’t beg.

She smiled.

And then she kicked the sack of money down the fire escape stairs.

Chapter 4

Haus der Presse smelled of ink and arrogance. The marble floors gleamed under chandeliers that dripped crystal like frozen tears. Meinhardt stood at the head of a long mahogany table, his back to the windows that overlooked the Spree. The river was a ribbon of oil under winter light.

His editors lounged in their seats, fingers drumming, eyes hungry. The mayor sat stiffly, his starched collar cutting into his jowls. Meinhardt raised a glass of champagne—acidic, too young, but the labels didn’t matter. Presentation did.

“Gentlemen,” he said, “today we toast not what we destroy, but what we preserve.”

A waiter circulated with trays of sandwiches cut into triangles. Meinhardt’s assistant, a woman with a face like a hatchet, placed a stack of pamphlets at each place setting.

Meinhardt tapped the top of his own stack. The first page read: *Berlin Purified: A Manifesto Against Moral Rot*.

“Distribute,” he ordered.

The editors reached for the pamphlets. Some flipped through the pages, nodding at the bold print, the damning photographs of sequined dresses and smudged lipstick. One editor paused at a photograph of Clara at the Moka, mid-pirouette, her arms spread wide. The caption read: *The Empress of Excess*.

Meinhardt caught the editor’s eye. “You recognize her?”

The editor swallowed. “Only by reputation.”

“A reputation that ends today.” Meinhardt lowered his voice, though the table was empty of outsiders. “The cabarets are cancer. They bleed this city dry. And Clara Voss? She was the metastasis.”

The mayor wiped his mouth with a napkin, leaving a smudge of rouge on the linen. “Your timing is impeccable, Klaus. The public is ready for change.”

Meinhardt smiled. “Public appetite is my métier.”

The assistant returned with a silver tray. On it, a single sheet of paper, folded into a square. She handed it to Meinhardt. His fingers didn’t tremble as he unfolded it, but his jaw tightened.

He crumpled the note in his fist.

The table stilled.

Meinhardt tossed the ball of paper onto a nearby sideboard. It bounced, unrolled slightly, revealing a single line in hasty script: *Your niece’s violin case is missing.*

Silence held for three heartbeats. Then a editor laughed, a sound like a broken glass. “Nephew trouble, Klaus? At your age?”

Meinhardt ignored him. He stared at the windows, at the river beyond, at the city that thought itself his to command.

Then he stood.

The chair legs scraped the floor. The mayor flinched.

“Gentlemen,” Meinhardt said, voice low, “excuse me.”

He strode to the door, his heels sharp on the marble. The assistant hurried after him, her heels clicking like a metronome.

Meinhardt didn’t look back. He didn’t need to.

The door slammed behind him.

In the hallway, he turned to his assistant. “Find out who took it.”

She nodded, already reaching for a telephone on the wall.

Meinhardt’s fingers drummed against his thigh. His mind wasn’t on the ledger. It wasn’t on the cabarets. It was on the past, on a night twenty years ago when a violin had fallen silent in a Berlin apartment, and a girl had run away weeping.

He hated loose ends.

The elevator arrived. Meinhardt stepped inside, the doors closing behind him with a quiet *shush*. The assistant’s voice faded as the car descended.

The elevator stopped. The doors opened.

Meinhardt stepped into the lobby.

A woman stood there, her back to him, adjusting her gloves. She wore a coat of deep navy, the collar turned up against the draft. Her hair was pinned severely, but a single curl escaped, catching the light like a copper wire.

She turned.

Lotte Weber.

Meinhardt’s niece.

Her violin case was slung over her shoulder. It looked empty.

Meinhardt’s breath caught. Not from fear. From recognition.

The past had found him.

Lotte saw him. Her lips parted in a smile that didn’t reach her eyes.

“Uncle Klaus,” she said. “I was hoping to catch you.”

Meinhardt stepped forward. The distance between them was less than a step.

Then he stopped.

Lotte’s hand rested on her coat pocket. The pocket bulged slightly, as if something inside it couldn’t decide whether to stay or go.

Meinhardt’s gaze flicked to the bulge. Then back to her face.

“You have something of mine,” he said.

Lotte’s smile widened. It was the smile of a woman holding a full house.

“I have something you need,” she said.

Meinhardt’s fingers twitched at his side.

The lobby was empty except for them. The doorman had stepped away. The reception desk was unattended.

Lotte took a step closer.

“You closed the Moka,” she said. “You framed Clara. You think you’ve won.”

Meinhardt didn’t blink. “I know I have.”

Lotte’s free hand came up, brushing a strand of hair from her face. Her fingers were long. Her nails were painted the color of dried blood.

“Then why,” she said, “are you trembling?”

Chapter 5

The Golden Rooster wasn’t just a club. It was a fortress of velvet and marble, a cavern of smoke and gold leaf that had survived wars and depressions. At three in the afternoon, the place should’ve been empty. Instead, it was a pressure cooker.

Meinhardt’s bodyguards pushed through the front doors, their coats straining over shoulder holsters. The doorman, a hulking ex-prizefighter named Heinz, stood with his arms crossed, blocking the coat check.

“Closed,” he said.

One of the guards—Kurt, according to his lapel pin—shoved Heinz against the wall. The doorman didn’t flinch. His eyes stayed on Meinhardt, waiting for the order.

Meinhardt smoothed his gloves over his fingers. “Cut the power. Smash the bar. Empty the till.”

Heinz’s jaw tightened. “You shut us down, Klaus. That’s one thing. But you don’t get to trash the place.”

Meinhardt smiled. It wasn’t a kind expression. “Then you should’ve picked a side sooner.”

Kurt drove a fist into Heinz’s stomach. The doorman gasped, folding like a broken chair. The second guard, a woman named Greta with a scar through one eyebrow, yanked the register drawer open and hurled trays of silverware across the room. Forks clattered against mirrors, shattering glass.

Meinhardt stepped over Heinz’s legs and walked deeper into the club. The air smelled of stale perfume and spilled whiskey. The stage curtains were still drawn, the velvet heavy with dust. He paused at the bar, running a finger along the mahogany. The varnish came away sticky.

A voice cut through the noise. “You’re making a mistake.”

Meinhardt turned. A woman stood in the doorway to the kitchen, her arms crossed under a stained apron. Fritzi, the head cook. Her hands were red from scrubbing, her knuckles cracked.

“Funny,” Meinhardt said. “I don’t remember asking.”

Fritzi’s eyes flicked to the guards, then back to him. “You closed the Moka. You framed Clara. You think this’ll stop the music?”

Greta raised a bottle of schnapps, aiming for the chandelier.

“Don’t,” Meinhardt said.

Greta hesitated.

Fritzi didn’t. She lunged, grabbing a cleaver from the counter. The blade caught the light as she swung. Greta yelped, stumbling back as the cleaver embedded itself in her shoulder. The gun clattered to the floor.

Kurt drew his pistol. Fritzi didn’t run. She stood her ground, breathing hard, the cleaver still in her grip.

Meinhardt sighed. “How many times must I explain? Power isn’t in the kitchen.”

Fritzi spat on the floor. “Power’s in the hands that feed the city.”

Then the lights went out.

Not just dimmed. Not just flickered. The entire building plunged into blackness so complete it felt like drowning. The air hummed with the sound of confused movement—boots scraping, voices cursing, a body hitting the floor with a thud.

“Torches,” Meinhardt ordered.

Greta groped for the bar’s emergency lantern, her breath coming in ragged gasps. The flame flickered to life, casting shadows that twisted like living things.

Fritzi was gone.

The kitchen door swung shut behind her.

Kurt kicked over a stool. “She’s getting out. We need to—”

A whistle split the dark. High and sharp. It came from the stage.

Meinhardt’s head snapped toward the sound.

The stage lights flared on.

The performers were there. Every last one of them. The chorus girls in tattered sequins, the trumpet player with his horn still in hand, the piano man with his fingers poised over the keys as if he’d never stopped playing. And at the front, Lotte Weber in a shimmering green dress, her violin tucked under her chin.

She drew the bow across the strings.

The first note was a challenge.

The second was defiance.

The third was war.

The crowd surged in from the back of the club—dockworkers, street kids, politicians who’d been drinking there for years and couldn’t bear to see it go. They pressed forward, pushing the bodyguards back, their voices rising in a single roar.

“Lotte! Lotte! Lotte!”

Meinhardt’s wife was in the middle of it.

Ilse Meinhardt stood near the piano, her fur stole slipping off one shoulder, her face flushed with something between excitement and terror. She saw her husband and waved, a little too eagerly.

Meinhardt pushed through the crowd, his shoulder knocking into a dancer’s elbow. The woman snarled but didn’t step aside. The music swelled, drowning out everything but the beat of blood in his ears.

Ilse grabbed his arm as he reached her. “Klaus, for God’s sake, we have to—”

“Not now,” he snapped.

Her lower lip trembled. “The exits are blocked. The fire doors are chained from the outside.”

Meinhardt’s stomach dropped.

Lotte’s violin shrieked, a sound like tearing metal. The crowd moved with it, a single organism, swaying, shouting. The chandeliers trembled.

Ilse’s hand tightened on his sleeve. “Klaus.”

He looked at the stage. At Lotte. At the way she played with her eyes half-closed, as if she were in another world. As if she’d already won.

The bartender appeared behind Meinhardt, his apron stained with wine and blood. He leaned in close, his voice tight.

“You closed the wrong club.”

The words didn’t make sense at first. Then Meinhardt’s gaze flicked to the stage again—and the realization hit like a boot to the ribs.

The Moka was gone. The Rusty Nail was next. But The Golden Rooster?

The Golden Rooster was where the Syndicate had always met.

The Golden Rooster was where the real power still lived.

Lotte’s bow came down in a final slash. The last note hung in the air like a blade poised over his throat.

Then the ceiling sprinklers burst.

Water rained down in sheets, soaking through Meinhardt’s coat, plastering his hair to his skull. The crowd shrieked, scattering like roaches. Ilse coughed, her hands clawing at his arm.

Meinhardt wrenched free, his gaze locked on Lotte as she lowered her violin.

She didn’t look at him. She looked past him, to the doors.

Where the first fire truck’s siren wailed in the distance.

Where the night was just beginning.

Chapter 6

The bank was a mausoleum of mahogany and marble, its floors polished to a mirror sheen, its air thick with the scent of old money. Clara Voss adjusted her gloves, the leather creaking like a conscience she no longer owned. The clerk behind the desk had a face like a wet sponge—round, pale, and smelling faintly of mint. He didn’t look up as she approached.

“Frau Voss,” he said, tapping a pen against a ledger. “We’ve been expecting you.”

“Have you.” Her voice was flat. A statement, not a question.

The clerk’s fingers stilled. He finally met her gaze, his eyes flicking to the envelope in her hand. “Cash transfer, as discussed?”

Clara slid the envelope across the desk. The clerk didn’t touch it. He leaned back in his chair, lacing his fingers over his stomach. “Wire instructions are already in the system. Three banks. Standard routing.”

“Timed for the night before the Press Ball.”

“Of course.” The clerk finally picked up the envelope, weighing it like a bad habit. “Though I must ask—why so public? Shell accounts are meant to be invisible.”

“They are.” Clara tapped the envelope once, her nail clicking against the paper. “This isn’t a bribe. It’s an invitation.”

The clerk’s eyebrows lifted. He opened the envelope, thumbing through the cash inside. His fingers paused. He pulled out a single sheet of paper, its edges ragged. A ledger page. His name was printed at the top: *Kurt Weber, ordering print run: 20,000 copies, Berliner Morgenpost, 12/12.*

The clerk’s throat worked. He folded the page slowly, carefully. “Frau Voss, I—”

“Keep the bonus.” Clara turned to leave. “And that page. Burn it after you’ve read it.”

The clerk didn’t move. “What if I don’t?”

Clara paused at the door. “Then you’ll learn the hard way why no one crosses the Empress of the Moka.”

She didn’t wait for his reaction.

---

The Rusty Nail was quiet at noon—a rare stretch of daylight in a city built for night. Clara pushed through the door, the hinges groaning like a tired man settling down for a long sleep. The dive bar smelled of spilled beer and old cigarettes, the kind of place where dreams went to die and sometimes came back as ghosts.

Rusty was behind the bar, polishing glasses with a rag that had seen better days. He didn’t look up as she approached.

“You’re early,” he grunted.

“You’re drunk,” she countered.

Rusty set the glass down. “Not yet.”

Clara slid onto a stool. The wood groaned beneath her. “The safe’s broken. Again.”

Rusty exhaled through his nose, a sound like a deflating balloon. “You gonna fix it?”

“For a price.”

“Your price is always high.”

“And your bills are always late.”

Rusty wiped his hands on his apron, leaving streaks of grime. He tossed the rag aside and shuffled toward the back room. Clara followed, her heels clicking against the sticky floor. The safe was a hulking thing, its dial frozen at the wrong number. Rusty fiddled with it, his breath sour.

Clara’s fingers brushed the cold metal. She turned the dial left, then right, then left again. The mechanism clicked. Rusty’s jaw dropped.

“Son of a bitch,” he muttered.

Clara stepped back. “There. Now it works.”

Rusty didn’t take his eyes off the safe. “How’d you—?”

“Magic.” Clara turned to leave.

“Wait.”

She paused.

Rusty scratched his stubble. “You ever think Meinhardt’s got it wrong?”

Clara stilled. “About what?”

Rusty shrugged, but his hands were tight on the bar. “About you. About all of it.” His gaze flicked to the door, then back. “I’ve seen the papers. The Moka’s gone. The Golden Rooster’s next. But you—” He shook his head. “You don’t strike me as the type who just lets things happen.”

Clara studied him. Rusty wasn’t an ally. He wasn’t a threat. He was just a man who had seen too much and decided to look away. Until now.

“Men like Meinhardt,” she said quietly, “don’t make mistakes. They make examples.”

Rusty’s mouth twisted. “Then what are you?”

Clara smiled. It wasn’t kind. “Something they haven’t invented a word for yet.”

She walked out before he could answer.

---

The street outside was a river of noise—cars honking, vendors shouting, a jazz trumpet scraping against the sky. Clara ducked into an alley, her heels splashing through a puddle of questionable origin. The alley was narrow, the walls slick with rain and grime. She pulled a cigarette from her coat, lighting it with a match that burned too bright in the dim.

A shadow moved. Too quick. Too deliberate.

Clara didn’t turn. “You’re late.”

Viktor Blau stepped into the dim light, his hands tucked into his pockets, his coat collar turned up like a man trying to disappear. “You know I don’t like being watched.”

“Then stop being predictable.” Clara exhaled smoke. “You tipped off Meinhardt.”

Viktor’s jaw tightened. “I protected you.”

“By handing him the noose.” Clara crushed the cigarette under her heel. “The safe was broken. Rusty just asked me if Meinhardt was wrong about me. Coincidence?”

“You think this is a game?” Viktor’s voice was low, dangerous. “He would’ve ruined you anyway. At least this way—”

“At least this way he sees me coming.” Clara stepped closer. “You wanted me to be the victim. You forgot I don’t do victims.”

Viktor’s eyes flickered—just once—toward the street behind her. “You’re playing with fire.”

Clara laughed. It was a dry, brittle sound. “I’ve been playing with fire since the first night I walked into the Moka. You just didn’t notice.”

A car horn blared in the distance. Viktor’s shoulders tensed. “He’s coming for you, Clara. Not the clubs. Not the money. You.”

Clara didn’t answer. She knew.

The alley’s far end filled with the rhythmic thud of boots on pavement. Three men, all in dark coats, all moving with the kind of precision that made their purpose clear. Viktor’s hand twitched in his pocket.

Clara turned her back on him. “Run.”

Viktor didn’t move. “Clara—”

“Run, Viktor.”

He ran.

The first man reached the alley’s mouth. He was tall, his face sharp enough to cut glass. He stopped when he saw her, his hand resting on the grip of his pistol.

“Frau Voss,” he said. “Herr Meinhardt would like a word.”

Clara smiled. “Tell him I’m busy.”

The man’s smile was a knife unsheathed. “He won’t like that.”

Chapter 7

The Rusty Nail had always been a place where bad luck and worse decisions gathered like flies on spoiled milk. But noontime was its most honest hour—no music, no drunks crying into their whiskey, just the slow drip of condensation from the beer taps and Rusty bitching at the world in general.

Clara pushed through the door and the hinges whined like a man who’d given up on tomorrow. Rusty didn’t look up from the glass he was polishing, his rag gray with generations of spilled lager.

“You’re early,” he said.

“You’re drunk,” she replied.

“Not yet.”

She slid onto a stool. The wood sighed under her weight. The drunk sailor at the far end of the bar was trying to drink a beer without a glass. He muttered something about U-boats and surrender, then dropped his head onto the counter with a wet thud.

Clara tapped the bar. “Safe’s broken.”

Rusty set the glass down carefully, like it might break if he so much as breathed on it. “Then fix it.”

“For a price.”

“Your prices are always highway robbery.”

“And your tabs are always in arrears.”

Rusty wiped his hands on his apron, leaving dark streaks across the fabric. He shuffled toward the back room without another word. Clara followed, her heels clicking against a floor that hadn’t been swept since the Kaiser abdicated.

The safe was a hulking beast, its dial frozen at the wrong number. Rusty spun the combination wheel idly, his frustration coming out in the form of muttered curses about “princesses who couldn’t take a hint.”

Clara’s fingers brushed the cold metal. She turned the dial left, then right, then left again. The mechanism clicked. Rusty’s hands stilled.

“Son of a bitch,” he said.

Clara stepped back. “Now it works.”

Rusty didn’t take his eyes off the safe. “How the hell did you—?”

“A gift.” She turned to leave.

“Wait.”

She paused.

Rusty scratched his stubble, leaving red marks on his weathered skin. “You ever think Meinhardt’s got it wrong?”

Clara stilled. “About me?”

Rusty shrugged, but his knuckles were white around the rag. “About all of it. Papers say the Moka’s gone. Golden Rooster next. But you—” He shook his head. “You don’t strike me as the type who just lets things happen.”

Clara studied him. Rusty wasn’t an ally. He wasn’t a threat. He was a man who’d seen too much and decided to look away. Until now.

“Men like Meinhardt,” she said quietly, “don’t make mistakes. They make examples.”

Rusty’s mouth twisted. “Then what are you?”

Clara smiled. It wasn’t kind. “Something they haven’t invented a word for yet.”

She walked out before he could answer.

The alley behind the bar smelled of wet cardboard and stale fries. Viktor Blau leaned against the brick wall, smoking, his coat collar turned up like he was trying to disappear into the shadows.

“You’re late,” Clara said.

“You’re observant.” He flicked ash onto the pavement. “You know they’re watching the Rusty now.”

“Let them watch.”

Viktor exhaled smoke. “He’s not going to stop at the clubs. He wants you.”

“He can’t have what he doesn’t understand.” Clara lit a cigarette with a match that flared too bright in the dim. “You tipped him off.”

Viktor’s jaw tightened. “To save you.”

“By giving him the rope.” She crushed the cigarette under her heel. “Rusty just asked me if Meinhardt was wrong about me. Coincidence?”

Viktor’s hand twitched in his pocket. “I did what I thought was right.”

“Right for whom?” Clara stepped closer. “You wanted me to be the victim. You forgot I don’t do victims.”

The distant wail of a police siren cut through the alley. Viktor’s eyes flicked toward the street, then back. “He’s coming for you, Clara. Not the money. You.”

Clara didn’t answer.

Viktor’s shoulders tensed. “Get out of the city. Tonight.”

Clara exhaled smoke. “I have a bar to run.”

Viktor’s voice dropped to a growl. “This isn’t a game.”

“Never said it was.”

The siren grew louder. Closer. Viktor’s hand came out of his pocket, holding a pistol. He didn’t point it. Didn’t need to.

Clara turned her back on him. “Run.”

Viktor didn’t move. “Clara—”

“Run, Viktor.”

He ran.

The first man stepped into the alley’s mouth. Tall. Thin. His coat too clean for this neighborhood. His hand rested on the grip of his pistol.

“Frau Voss,” he said. “Herr Meinhardt would like a word.”

Clara smiled. “Tell him I’m busy.”

The man’s smile was a blade unsheathed. “He won’t like that.”

Clara’s fingers tightened around her cigarette case. “Then he’ll learn disappointment the hard way.”

The man’s eyes flicked to the cigarette case, then to her face. His hand came off the pistol.

Clara’s case clicked open.

Inside wasn’t a cigarette.

The man’s smile vanished. He took a step back.

Clara closed the case with a snap.

“Run,” she said.

Chapter 8

The Haus der Presse smelled of printer’s ink and bad decisions. Klaus Meinhardt stood in the elevator, his jaw tight enough to crack walnuts, his morning coat straining over shoulders that had not slept. The brass doors groaned shut behind him. The operator, a fresh-faced kid who smelled of hair tonic and fear, didn’t meet his eyes.

“Which floor, sir?”

“Basement,” Meinhardt said.

The kid swallowed. “All the editors called in sick today.”

“Good. Sick of me.”

The elevator lurched downward. Meinhardt smoothed his cuffs, though the crisp linen was already flawless. The ledger had been a mistake. Not the bribes—those were necessary, inevitable, the cost of progress. But the ledger itself, with its careful columns of names and figures, had become a weapon in the hands of his enemies. His wife had filed for divorce before noon. His board had convened an emergency session. His brother-in-law had called to ask if he wanted his lawyers to “handle it quietly.”

No.

He wanted blood.

The doors opened onto a corridor that smelled of ammonia and old fear. Meinhardt stepped out, boots clicking against the concrete. His assistant, a harried woman with ink-stained fingers, scurried toward him.

“Herr Meinhardt, the staff—”

“Have deserted me. I know.” He brushed past her. “Send the lawyers to my wife. Tell them to bring the prenup.”

She hesitated. “And the reporters?”

“What reporters?”

“All of them. They’re outside the building. They’re calling it a walkout.”

Meinhardt stopped. Turned. “And the presses?”

“Still.”

He exhaled through his nose. “Then we print.”

---

The pawnshop on Oranienstraße was a cave of forgotten sins. Instruments leaned against the walls like convalescents, their varnish dull under the sickly yellow light of a single bulb. A clarinet, its keys tarnished green, lay atop a stack of sheet music. Someone had scribbled on the margins in pencil—notes, not lyrics. Scribbles by a drunk or a genius.

The pawnbroker, a skeletal man with a waxed mustache and fingers that looked like they’d never held anything but cash, didn’t look up when Meinhardt entered. He was examining a silver cigarette case, turning it in the light like a priest blessing a chalice.

“Closed,” he said.

“I need information.”

The man set the case down. “I deal in goods, not secrets.”

“Goods have histories.” Meinhardt dropped a twenty on the counter. The bill didn’t flutter. It landed like a stone. “Violin case. Dark wood. Initials carved inside. Someone bought it back yesterday.”

The pawnbroker’s eyes flicked to the money. Then to Meinhardt’s face. Then away.

“Class act,” he said. “Real class act. Came in with gloves on. Didn’t haggle.”

“Description.”

“Blonde. Tall. Wore a coat that cost more than this shop.”

Meinhardt’s pulse thudded in his throat. “Lotte.”

The pawnbroker didn’t react. Just picked up the cigarette case again. “You want it back?”

“No.” Meinhardt turned toward the door. “I want to know where she went.”

The man chuckled, a dry sound like parchment tearing. “You’re the press baron, aren’t you? The one cleaning up Berlin?”

Meinhardt didn’t look back. “And you’re the one who’ll live to see tomorrow if you tell me.”

The laughter stopped.

---

The Golden Rooster was empty at three in the afternoon, the plush seats sagging under the weight of absence, the stage a gaping maw of black velvet. Lotte Weber stood in the wings, her bow in hand, her fingers tracing the edge of a violin case that no longer belonged to her. She had pawned it the night before—needed the cash, needed the anonymity. But the pawnbroker’s words had followed her home like a bad smell.

*Some dame bought it back—real class act.*

She should have kept the case. Should have burned it. But the sheet music inside was Meinhardt’s undoing, and she had carried it too far already.

A hand clamped over her mouth.

She kicked back, her heel connecting with shinbone. A grunt. The grip loosened just enough for her to twist free.

Meinhardt stood there, his face a mask of controlled rage, his coat flaring like the wings of a carrion bird.

“You,” he said.

Lotte backed up, her spine hitting the velvet curtain. The stage lights were cold. Harsh. They turned his face into something inhuman.

“Uncle,” she said, because what else was there?

He stepped closer. “Did you think I wouldn’t notice?”

“I didn’t think you’d care.”

His hand shot out, grabbed her wrist. His fingers were cold. Unforgiving. “The ledger. Where is it?”

Lotte swallowed. “I don’t know what you’re talking about.”

Meinhardt’s laugh was a knife dragged across glass. “You’re my blood, girl. You inherited my obstinacy. But not my patience.” He leaned in, his breath sour with coffee and cigarettes. “Tell me where it is, or I will ruin you so thoroughly your own mother won’t recognize the ashes.”

Lotte’s fingers twitched toward the violin case at her feet. The one he hadn’t seen. The one that held everything.

“I sold it,” she said.

Meinhardt’s eyes narrowed. “To whom?”

“Does it matter?”

“Yes.” His voice dropped to a whisper. “Because the moment I find whoever took it, they will tell me everything. And then I will break their legs. Slowly.”

Lotte’s breath hitched. She thought of Clara Voss. Of the way the Empress of the Moka had looked at her the first night—like she saw something worth saving.

“You’re bluffing,” Lotte said.

Meinhardt’s grip tightened. “Am I?”

A door slammed somewhere backstage. Footsteps. Voices.

Lotte wrenched her arm free. “Ask the pawnbroker on Oranienstraße.”

Then she ran.

---

The pawnshop’s bell jingled like a broken wind chime as Lotte burst through the door, her chest heaving, her coat flapping behind her like the wings of a startled bird.

The pawnbroker looked up, his mustache twitching with irritation. “You again?”

Lotte didn’t stop. She skidded behind the counter, her gloved hands fumbling under the display case. The pawnbroker shouted, but she was already pulling out a cigar box, flipping it open.

Inside was a ledger. Small. Leather-bound. The pages were filled with dates, names, numbers. Every bribe Meinhardt had ever paid. Every promise he’d ever made.

She pressed it into the pawnbroker’s hands.

“Burn it,” she gasped. “Burn it all.”

Then she was out the door again, running into the afternoon glare of Berlin, her breath tearing through her lungs like a thing alive.

Behind her, the pawnbroker stared at the ledger. Then at her retreating back.

“Son of a bitch,” he muttered.

He lit a match.

chapters.title: **Chapter 9: The Offer**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 9: The Offer**

The Rusty Nail smelled like spilled beer and yesterday’s rain. Clara wiped a glass with a rag that had once been white. The rag was gray now, like everything in the place—gray and waiting for something to happen.

The door creaked. She didn’t look up.

Viktor Blau stepped inside, his hat already off, his coat already damp at the shoulders. Rain streaked his face, but it wasn’t from outside. He’d been running.

Clara set the glass down. “You’re late.”

He didn’t answer. Just pulled out a chair and sat, his shoes scuffing against the scarred wood. The stool groaned under his weight.

“Meinhardt wants you gone,” he said.

Clara picked up another glass. Filled it with gin. Pushed it across the bar. “I know.”

Viktor’s fingers twitched. “Not just gone. *Gone.* Berlin. Tonight.”

She took a sip of her own drink. The gin burned clean. “And if I don’t?”

“He’ll take the Moka. Then the Rusty. Then every joint you’ve ever touched.” Viktor leaned in, his voice low. “He’s not bluffing, Clara.”

She set the glass down. “Neither am I.”

A laugh bubbled up from the corner of the room—a drunk with a harmonica, playing something that wasn’t a song. Viktor’s eyes flicked toward him, then back.

“You don’t have to do this alone,” he said.

Clara wiped the bar. Slow. Deliberate. “I’m not doing it alone.”

Viktor’s jaw tightened. “You know what I mean.”

She did. Of course she did. Viktor had always thought he knew what was best for her. Same way he’d known it was best to tell Meinhardt about the ledger. Best to hand over the noose. Best to let the empire crumble.

Clara picked up a rag she didn’t need. Wiped the same spot three times.

“You had your chance to help,” she said. “You handed me to him.”

Viktor’s face darkened. “I was trying to protect you.”

“From what?”

“From *this.*” He gestured at the bar, the drunk, the dim light. “From burning alive.”

Clara tossed the rag aside. “I’m already burning.”

The harmonica player hit a sour note. Viktor ignored it.

“Meinhardt’s offering a deal,” he said. “Drop the charges. Walk away. He’ll even throw in a train ticket.”

“Where to?”

“Anywhere but here.”

Clara considered it. Zurich. Prague. Somewhere with clean air and no newspapers. Somewhere where Meinhardt’s reach couldn’t stretch.

Then she thought of the ledger Lotte Weber was carrying. Of the Syndicate’s slush fund, stashed in places even Viktor didn’t know. Of the Moka Elegance’s grand reopening, planned for the night of the Press Ball.

She smiled.

“No,” she said.

Viktor’s fingers curled into fists. “Clara—”

“I’m not running.”

“Then you’re dead.”

She leaned forward. “I’m already dead. Just not buried yet.”

The harmonica player gave up. The silence in the bar was thick enough to chew.

Viktor exhaled through his nose. “You’re impossible.”

Clara dipped her chin. “So are you. That’s why we worked.”

He stared at her. Really stared. For the first time in years, she saw the boy he’d been—before the debt collectors, before the brother’s blood on the floor. Before the world taught them both how to survive.

Then his voice cracked.

“You don’t have to do this alone.”

Clara poured herself another drink. Watched the amber liquid settle.

“Watch me,” she said.

chapters.title: **Chapter 10: The Scope**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 10: The Scope**

Clara’s apartment smelled of old perfume and newer smoke. She’d aired it out twice already, but the scent of cheap gin and older sins clung to the curtains.

The knock came at 3:17 a.m.

She didn’t answer right away. Just stood in the dark, listening to the rhythm of the knock—three slow, two quick. Viktor’s code.

By the time she reached the door, the knock had changed to a fist against wood.

She opened it.

A courier stood there, younger than she expected—twenty, maybe twenty-one, with a delivery pouch slung over one shoulder and a cigarette clamped between his teeth. The paper had yellowed at the edges.

“Clara Voss?” he asked, already sliding the pouch off.

She nodded.

He didn’t smile. Just held out the pouch.

She took it. The leather was warm from his body.

“Sign here,” he said, shoving a clipboard at her.

She scribbled her name without reading whatever he’d slapped on the page.

He tapped the clipboard with his pen. “That’s it.”

Then he was gone, his shoes scuffing against the hallway’s grimy carpet.

Clara shut the door. Locked it.

She carried the pouch to the kitchen table. Unbuckled the straps. Lifted the lid.

Inside was a ledger. Bound in cracked black leather, the kind used for accounting in a time before typewriters. The pages were thick, the handwriting precise—someone who took their bribes with receipts.

She didn’t open it. Not yet.

She set it aside. Poured a finger of whiskey into a chipped glass. Drank it neat.

Then she turned the ledger over.

Slid out a single sheet of paper.

Typed.

*Lotte Weber plays at the Moka Elegance tonight—alone.*

Clara folded the note in half. Tucked it into her coat pocket.

She carried the ledger to the bedroom. Knelt beside the false bottom of her wardrobe. Pressed her palm against the hidden latch. The panel slid open with a sigh.

She laid the ledger inside, next to the others.

One for the Syndicate’s cut.
One for the politicians who took it.
One for her brother’s name, still unpaid in blood and coin.

She closed the wardrobe. Straightened her skirt.

The Moka Elegance.

She hadn’t set foot in the place since Meinhardt’s men rolled through with their truncheons and search warrants. The chandeliers were probably still dusted with the remains of their raid—broken glass, shattered ashtrays, the kind of chaos that took weeks to clean up if you were lucky.

But tonight, someone was playing there. Alone.

Clara checked the clock. 3:42 a.m.

She went to the hall closet. Pulled out a suitcase. Not hers—never hers. Men’s wool, expensive, still smelling faintly of sandalwood cologne.

She unzipped it.

Inside was cash. Packs of it, wrapped in oilskin. Enough to buy a small war.

She zipped it shut.

Then she sat on the bed. Lit a cigarette. Watched the smoke curl toward the ceiling.

The Moka Elegance was a fortress now. Guarded, probably. Meinhardt wouldn’t want surprises.

But Lotte Weber was playing alone.

Clara exhaled smoke. Thought about the violin case under Viktor’s arm the night he’d come to the Rusty Nail. Thought about the way his fingers had trembled when he’d mentioned the ledger in her brother’s name.

She stubbed the cigarette out.

Five hours to get dressed. Five hours to get there.

She had a plan. A real one this time.

She grabbed her coat from the back of the chair. Slipped her arms in.

Then her phone rang.

Not her apartment’s landline—the old one that only Viktor knew. The one that hadn’t rung in years.

She let it go to voicemail.

The message alert pinged a second later.

Clara pulled the phone from her pocket. Didn’t listen. Just deleted the message.

She locked the apartment. Took the stairs down to the street.

The first tram wasn’t for another twenty minutes.

She walked.

chapters.title: **Chapter 11: The Freefall**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 11: The Freefall**

The Rusty Nail stank of stale beer and desperation, the kind of place where men came to forget their names let alone their problems. Clara wiped condensation from her glass with a rag that had long since given up trying to be white. The afternoon crowd was thin—two dockworkers hunched over a table of dice, a woman in a moth-eaten fur coat nursing something clear in a chipped glass.

The door swung open. Cold air rushed in, carried the smell of wet pavement and print ink.

Viktor Blau stood there, coat damp at the shoulders, face unshaven. He didn’t look at her right away. Just shut the door, shook water from his sleeves.

Clara didn’t pour him a drink.

“You look like hell,” she said.

Viktor exhaled through his nose. “You look like you’re planning a murder.”

“That’s because I am.”

He stepped closer, boots squeaking on the sticky floor. “Meinhardt’s printing plant burned to the ground last night. Four dead. Two of them his own men.”

Clara set the rag down. “Was it an accident?”

“They’re saying arson.” Viktor’s fingers drummed the bar. “His wife just testified before the commission. Said he’s been beating her for years. Said he’s got a mistress who’s half his age. Said he’s got a ledger full of names.”

“Did she mention cabarets?”

“She mentioned corruption.” Viktor leaned in. “Clara, Meinhardt’s losing his grip. If you go after him now—”

“He framed me. Ruined my brother. Stole everything.” She picked up a bottle of schnapps, poured two fingers into a glass. Didn’t offer Viktor any. “You tipped him off. You let him burn my empire.”

Viktor’s jaw tightened. “I thought it would be quick. Clean. You’d walk away with your life.”

“My life?” Clara laughed, low and sharp. “You didn’t think I’d fight?”

A muscle twitched in Viktor’s cheek. “I thought you’d run.”

Clara slid the glass toward him. “Drink.”

He didn’t move.

She drank hers. Let the burn settle in her throat. “Where’s Lotte Weber?”

Viktor’s eyes flicked to the door. “You don’t want to—”

“Where is she?”

“Behind the Golden Rooster. Drunk. Crying into her violin case.” He rubbed his temples. “She showed up there last night with a man Meinhardt sent. He took the ledger. Broke her fingers doing it.”

Clara’s glass hit the bar. “She’s alive?”

“Barely.”

Clara was already moving.

---

The alley behind the Golden Rooster reeked of fried onions and cigarette ash. A neon sign flickered overhead—ROOSTER in red, the rest burned out. Lotte Weber sat on a crate, coat pooled around her, one hand cradled against her chest. The other gripped a broken violin bow like a knife.

Clara stepped into the dim light.

Lotte’s head snapped up. Eyes red, mascara streaking. “You shouldn’t be here.”

“I could say the same.”

Lotte laughed, wet and broken. “Uncle Klaus is going to kill me for this.”

“He already tried.” Clara crouched in front of her. “What happened to your case?”

Lotte’s fingers tightened on the bow. “He sent his man. Big. German shepherd at his heels. They took it. Tore it apart right there on the street.”

Clara’s pulse thrummed in her throat. “Did they get what was inside?”

Lotte’s laugh turned ugly. “They got the wrong thing.”

Clara went still. “What does that mean?”

Lotte pulled her coat aside. Her violin case lay cracked open, half the lining torn out. A single sheet of paper fluttered from a splintered seam. Clara plucked it free.

Printed in neat type:

*To the Honorable Klaus Meinhardt*
*Re: Undisclosed assets held in trust via H. Voss Properties*
*Balance as of 1929: 470,000 Reichsmarks*

No signature. No date. Just Meinhardt’s name at the top like a target.

Lotte’s voice was raw. “He thinks he has the ledger. But he doesn’t.”

Clara folded the paper. Slid it into her pocket.

“You stole from him,” Clara said.

“He stole from me first.” Lotte’s breath hitched. “He ruined my father. Drove him to drink. Then he took his name, his business, his goddamn violin.”

Clara reached out. Touched Lotte’s wrist. Not her injured hand.

Lotte flinched but didn’t pull away.

“You’re bleeding,” Clara said.

Lotte looked at her fingers. Fresh red welts across her knuckles. “It’s nothing.”

Clara stood. Held out her hand.

Lotte stared at it. Then at Clara.

“You’re offering me a way out,” Lotte said.

Clara didn’t answer.

Lotte’s laugh was bitter. “He’ll come for me anyway.”

“Not if he’s the one on trial.” Clara’s fingers curled slightly. “Not if the ledger is real.”

Lotte’s breath hitched. “You have it?”

Clara didn’t answer.

Lotte’s gaze flicked to the door of the Golden Rooster, then back to Clara. Something flickered in her eyes—hope, fear, the dangerous edge of wanting to believe.

A shadow moved at the alley’s mouth.

A detective in a rumpled trench coat stepped into the dim light, hat in hand. “Frau Weber?”

Lotte’s grip on the bow tightened.

The detective held up a cigarette case. Silver. Engraved.

*C.V.*

His eyes flicked to Clara. “Recognize this?”

Clara didn’t move. Didn’t breathe.

The detective snapped the case open. Inside, a single page—creased, smudged. Handwriting that wasn’t hers, but unmistakable in its intent.

*Klaus Meinhardt owes the Berlin Syndicate 470,000 Reichsmarks. Payable upon delivery of the ledger carrying his name.*

The detective’s voice was clinical. “Found this in the ashes of Meinhardt’s printing plant. Along with your initials.”

Clara’s palm itched. She’d left that ledger page in her coat pocket. Had Viktor taken it? Or had Meinhardt planted it?

The detective closed the case. Slipped it into his coat. “Mind answering a few questions downtown?”

Lotte’s breath came fast. “She didn’t do anything.”

The detective ignored her. His gaze stayed on Clara.

Clara looked past him, at the neon sign flickering above the Golden Rooster. Then back at the detective.

She reached into her coat. Not for a gun. For the engagement ring she’d taken off years ago.

She set it on the crate between them.

“Call Viktor Blau,” Clara said. “Tell him Clara Voss is ready to talk.”

The detective’s eyes flicked to the ring. Recognition flared. Viktor’s ring. Viktor’s mess.

Clara smiled. Not a kind smile.

“Tell him,” she repeated, “I’m coming for him.”

The detective’s hand went to his notepad. “Frau Voss—”

Clara walked past him. Didn’t look back.

Lotte called after her, voice raw. “Wait—”

Clara didn’t stop.

The detective’s voice cut through the alley’s damp silence. “Where are you going?”

Clara didn’t answer.

She was already out of the alley. The streetlights blurred. The tram rattled past, full of laughing strangers she’d never see again.

She walked faster. The Moka Elegance was still standing. Still hers, if she wanted it.

And Meinhardt was going to learn what happened when you burned a woman’s empire to the ground.

She turned the corner.

A squad car’s engine growled to life behind her.

Chapter 12

The Moka Elegance had been dark for weeks. Boarded windows like dead men’s eyelids. Dust swirled in the lobby where once champagne flutes had clinked against marble. Clara pushed through the front door, the lock clicking open with a sound like a gunshot.

Inside, the air smelled of mildew and old roses.

Klaus Meinhardt stood in the center of the grand hall, his back to her, adjusting his cufflinks beneath the shattered crystal of the chandelier. His suit was charcoal, his shoes polished enough to reflect the dying light. A half-empty bottle of brandy sat on the stage where his wife had once sung. He hadn’t heard her come in.

Clara stepped over the threshold. The door swung shut behind her with a sigh.

Meinhardt turned. His eyebrows lifted—polite surprise, practiced indifference.

“Clara,” he said. “I heard you were working at some dive bar. Rusty something or other.”

“Rusty Nail.” She walked down the aisle, heels clicking on the worn carpet. “They let me pour champagne. But not the good kind.”

“How fortunate.” He poured himself another finger of brandy. “I’d offer you some, but I suspect you’re not here for drinks.”

She stopped three rows from the stage. Close enough to see the gold filling in his left molar. Close enough to smell the lavender in his handkerchief.

“You burned my printing plant,” she said.

Meinhardt swirled the brandy. “Arson investigation is ongoing.”

“You sent men to Lotte’s apartment.”

“My niece is troubled.” He took a slow sip. “She’s been stealing. From me. From the Syndicate. From her own family.”

“Your name is on every page of that ledger.”

He set the glass down. “What ledger?”

Clara reached into her coat. Not fast. Not threatening. Just a slow unbuttoning, a slow reach. She pulled out the sheet from the detective’s cigarette case and tossed it onto the stage.

It fluttered down like a dead leaf.

Meinhardt didn’t move to pick it up. “That’s a forgery.”

“Is it?” Clara stepped onto the stage. Her boot heels echoed in the empty hall. “Look at the balance. 470,000 Reichsmarks. The same amount you just declared in taxes. Convenient.”

Meinhardt’s smile didn’t waver. “You were always good with numbers, Clara. But you forget—I control the ledgers now. I control the press. I control what Berlin believes.”

Clara walked past him, toward the wings. She flicked on the stage lights. The bulbs buzzed, then flared to painful brightness. Gold flecks danced in the air.

And then Clara saw them.

Syndicate enforcers stood along the walls—Fritzi with a sawn-off beneath her coat, Kurt Weber leaning against the curtain, arms crossed. Even old Herr Bauer, who ran the card games at the Golden Rooster, stood there with a Colt in his hand.

Meinhardt’s gaze followed hers. His jaw tightened.

“You think you can walk in here and take what’s mine?” he asked.

Clara didn’t answer. She walked to the center of the stage, where Lotte Weber had played her final set before the place was shuttered. She pulled a thick envelope from her coat and tossed it onto the piano.

“Inside,” Clara said, “are copies. Mailed this morning to every editor in Berlin who still has a spine. To the commissioner. To the Reichstag members who like their coffee strong and their stories sharper.”

Meinhardt didn’t touch the envelope. “You’re bluffing.”

Clara smiled. “No. I’m done hiding.”

She nodded to Fritzi.

The enforcers stepped forward. Not toward Meinhardt. Toward the proscenium arch, where two men in police uniforms stood holding crowbars. They pried at the wood paneling.

Meinhardt’s voice went thin. “What are you doing?”

Clara walked to the piano. Lifted the lid. Reached beneath the strings.

She pulled out a ledger.

Not a copy. The original. Leather-bound, gold-lettered. The spine cracked from use.

Meinhardt’s face went white.

“Every bribe,” Clara said. “Every payoff. Every promise you ever made. And there, on page 213, your signature next to Lotte’s father’s name. The night you drove him to jump off the Oberbaum Bridge.”

Meinhardt’s mouth opened. Closed.

Clara set the ledger on the piano. Opened it.

“You were going to be chancellor,” she said. “A cleaner Berlin. No cabarets. No jazz. No women like me.”

Meinhardt swallowed. “You don’t understand. The city is rotting from the inside. I was saving it.”

Clara flipped a page.

“From the inside,” she repeated.

Across the stage, the enforcers had pried off the back panel of the wall. Behind it: stacks of cash. Bundles wrapped in brown paper, stamped with the Reichsbank seal. Millions.

Meinhardt’s breath came shallow. “That’s Syndicate money.”

Clara nodded. “And every mark is accounted for in this ledger. Page 347 lists your political campaign donations. Page 352 lists the bribes you paid to have the arson charges dropped on your plant. Page 371 lists the price of your silence regarding Lotte’s father’s death.”

Meinhardt’s voice was a rasp. “You’re not Syndicate.”

“I am now.” Clara closed the ledger. “The Syndicate just voted. Unanimously. You’re out. The money’s seized. The venues revert to their original owners.”

Meinhardt’s hands trembled. “This isn’t legal.”

Clara stepped closer. Close enough to see the vein throbbing in his temple.

“Legal?” she said. “Klaus, you burned down a building full of people. You framed a woman for murder. You broke a girl’s fingers to steal a ledger. You think legality matters now?”

She reached out. Not to hit him. To touch the ledger.

His fingers twitched. Almost reached for it himself.

Clara’s voice dropped. Low. Soft.

“You were always the rot, Klaus. But you forgot—rot grows in the dark.”

Meinhardt’s face crumpled. Just for a second. A flicker of something human, something afraid.

Then the door at the back of the hall banged open.

Viktor Blau stood there, coat dripping rain, hands raised—not in surrender, but in defeat.

“Klaus,” Viktor said. “It’s over.”

Meinhardt didn’t take his eyes off Clara. “You. You set this up.”

Clara didn’t answer.

Meinhardt’s breath hitched. A sound like a trapped animal.

Then he lunged.

Not at Clara.

At the ledger.

His fingers closed around the leather cover. He wrenched it from the piano. The pages tore.

Clara didn’t move.

Meinhardt stared at the ruined book in his hands. The edges frayed. The ink bleeding like tears.

He looked at Clara.

“You were always the rot,” he said.

Chapter 13

Haus der Presse smelled of wet wool and old contracts. The lobby stretched long and narrow, lined with marble columns veined in gray. Security guards in pressed uniforms stood at attention, their eyes tracking movement like clock hands—mechanical, unblinking. Lotte Weber walked past them without stopping. Her violin case bumped against her thigh, the metal cold through her coat.

She didn’t look back.

The elevator doors opened before she reached them. A man in a homburg stepped out, adjusting his gloves. He recognized her. Nodded once—a silent acknowledgment. Not respect. Not kindness. Recognition of blood. He walked past without a word.

Lotte stepped into the elevator. The doors sealed behind her with a quiet hiss.

She didn’t press a button. Didn’t need to.

The penthouse level.

Klaus Meinhardt’s office took up the entire floor. Floor-to-ceiling windows framed the Spree, the water black and still beneath the city lights. Rain tapped against the glass like impatient fingers.

The receptionist didn’t look up when Lotte walked in. He kept typing, his fingers staccato on the keys. Typists lined the walls behind him, their faces pale in the fluorescent light. No one met her gaze.

Lotte didn’t ask for permission. She turned left, down the corridor lined with framed newspaper front pages—each one a Meinhardt byline, each headline sharper than the last. 1927: CORRUPTION IN THE NIGHTCLUBS. 1928: THE DECADENCE OF BERLIN’S ELITE. 1929: CLEAN HANDS FOR A NEW GERMANY.

She pushed open the double doors to his office.

Klaus Meinhardt sat behind a mahogany desk so large it could have been a banquet table. His back was to the door, his silhouette framed by the city’s glow. He didn’t turn around.

“You’re late,” he said.

“I wasn’t sure you’d see me.”

“Nonsense.” He swiveled in his chair. His face was clean-shaven, his jowls sagging slightly over a starched collar. “I always see you. That’s the problem.”

Lotte didn’t sit. She set the violin case on his desk.

Meinhardt’s gaze flicked to it. Not curiosity. Recognition.

“You took it again,” he said.

Lotte unlatched the case. Slow. Deliberate. The interior lined in worn velvet, the bow resting in a silk groove. She lifted the violin free. Ebony wood gleamed under the desk lamp.

She placed the case on the floor.

Meinhardt’s fingers drummed the desktop. Tap. Tap. Tap. Like a metronome counting down.

“You think this changes anything?” he asked.

Lotte lifted the bow. Fitted it to her fingers. Tested the tension with a soft *creak*.

“I don’t think,” she said. “I *know*.”

Meinhardt stood. His chair rolled back with a quiet scrape. He walked around the desk, his shoes silent on the carpet. He stopped a few feet away, close enough to smell the wisteria cologne he always wore—something from a garden that didn’t exist.

“You’re my niece,” he said. “That means something.”

Lotte didn’t answer. She tucked the violin under her chin. The first note was a whisper—a D, long and trembling. Then another. A G. Then a B-flat, low and resonant.

Meinhardt’s shoulders tensed. “What are you doing?”

Lotte ignored him. She played the opening of *Rhapsody in Blue*. Not the full piece. Just the first twelve bars. Simple. Familiar. A children’s lullaby compared to the fire she could unleash.

But it was enough.

Meinhardt’s face darkened. “Stop this.”

She played louder. The notes filled the room, bouncing off the glass, the wood, the marble. A solo voice in a cathedral of lies.

Meinhardt reached for her arm. Lotte sidestepped. Her bow never wavered.

She set the music down.

On the stand: sheets of paper. Printed ledger pages. Numbers in neat columns. Dates. Names.

Klaus Meinhardt’s name on every line.

She played the next notes softly, plucking the strings with her fingertips—*pizzicato*, like raindrops. Each note matched a date. Each date matched a bribe.

Meinhardt’s jaw clenched. “You think this is evidence? You’re a musician, Lotte. You don’t understand how the world works.”

She shifted the bow to her right hand. Used the left to flip a page. Another entry. Another bribe. Another name: *Lotte Weber’s father*.

Meinhardt’s breath hitched. Just once.

Lotte played that name too.

The violin sang the truth.

Meinhardt’s hand darted out. He grabbed for the violin.

Lotte twisted away. The bow slipped. The strings twanged. A discordant shriek filled the room.

Meinhardt froze.

His fingers had caught the violin. Not hard. Not violent. Just a grip—tense, desperate.

Lotte didn’t pull back. She just stared at his hand on her instrument.

Meinhardt realized what he’d done. His grip loosened. Almost apologetic.

Then he wrenched.

The violin tore free. A string snapped with a sharp *crack*. The bow clattered to the floor.

Lotte didn’t flinch. But her fingers trembled.

Meinhardt held the violin like a weapon. Like something he could break over his knee.

“You were always his favorite,” he said. “Claire or Clara or whatever she calls herself now. You think she’ll take you in? You think she’ll protect you? She’s using you. Just like she used your father.”

Lotte didn’t move. Her breath came steady. Only her eyes betrayed her—bright, wet, furious.

Meinhardt’s mouth twisted. “You’re nothing to her. Just a pawn. Just like you were to me.”

Lotte dropped the shattered bow.

She met his gaze. Direct. Unafraid.

Then she reached into her coat.

Meinhardt’s hand tightened on the violin. His knuckles turned white.

Lotte pulled out a crumpled handkerchief. Blotted her nose. Touched it to her eyes.

Meinhardt exhaled. Disgusted. Relieved.

She folded the cloth carefully. Slipped it back into her pocket.

Then she stepped forward.

Meinhardt braced himself. The violin still clutched in his hand.

Lotte reached out—not for the violin. For the ledger.

She plucked a single sheet from the stand.

Meinhardt lunged.

He didn’t get the violin. He got the paper.

Lotte’s fingers closed on empty air.

A paper cut stung her palm.

Meinhardt stared at the sheet in his hand. His face went slack.

The sheet was blank.

Lotte smiled. A slow, cold thing.

Then she turned and walked out.

Meinhardt roared. The sound shook the windows. The violin flew—slammed against the wall, splintering wood and snapping strings. He didn’t look at it. He stared at the door Lotte had just walked through.

His chest heaved. His face was red, veins standing out at his temples.

Then he grabbed the phone.

“Get her,” he barked. “Get her now.”

Chapter 14

The Rusty Nail’s backroom was a cave of cigarette haze and stale beer. Clara sat at a scarred oak table, her fingers drumming the wood in the rhythm of an old waltz. The light over the table flickered, casting her face in alternating shadows. She wore black—a simple dress, no jewelry, no trace of the Empress she’d once been. Just a woman waiting.

The door creaked open.

Viktor stepped inside, his hat already in hand. He didn’t look at her at first. Closed the door. Turned the deadbolt. Only then did his eyes meet hers, gray and tired.

"You’re letting him live," he said.

Clara didn’t answer. She poured two glasses of schnapps from the bottle between them. Pushed one toward him.

Viktor stayed standing. "He’ll come back. He always does."

Clara lifted her glass. "Not this time."

Viktor’s jaw tightened. "You don’t know that."

She took a slow sip. The alcohol burned clean. "I know his kind. Meinhardt doesn’t fight fair. He doesn’t play by rules. So I changed the game."

Viktor exhaled sharply. "You’re giving him what he wants. A clean exit. A chance to rebuild."

Clara set her glass down. "No. I’m giving him exactly what he deserves."

A knock at the door. Three short raps. Viktor stiffened. Clara didn’t move.

"Enter," she said.

The door opened. Klaus Meinhardt stood in the frame, his coat damp from the rain outside. He didn’t smile. Didn’t nod. Just stepped inside like he owned the place—which, once, he had.

Viktor’s fingers twitched at his side. Clara held up a hand. A silent order.

Meinhardt shut the door behind him. His gaze flicked from Viktor to Clara. "You wanted to see me."

Clara gestured to the chair across from her. Meinhardt didn’t sit. Viktor didn’t move.

"You tried to ruin me," Clara said. "Not just my venues. My name. My life. You wanted me broken. Silent. Erased."

Meinhardt adjusted his cuffs. "And you’re not broken?"

"Not even close."

He smiled. Thin, humorless. "Then you’ve wasted my time."

Clara leaned forward. "I’m offering you exile. Tonight. A train to Amsterdam. No questions. No interference. You walk away—permanently."

Meinhardt’s eyes flicked to Viktor. "And him?"

"He stays."

Meinhardt laughed. A dry, brittle sound. "You think I fear Viktor? He’s already chosen you. Again."

Viktor’s face darkened. "I chose to protect you."

Clara cut in. "You chose to protect yourself. Now you’ll choose exile."

Meinhardt studied her. The weight of his gaze was a physical thing, pressing against her skin. "Why?"

"Because I have what you want," Clara said.

She reached into her coat. Meinhardt’s hand went to his pocket—instinct, habit. Clara pulled out a sheet of paper. Folded once. Handed it across the table.

Meinhardt took it. Unfolded it. His face went still.

The paper was blank.

He looked up. "This is a joke."

Clara shook her head. "No. It’s an ultimatum. Leave Berlin. Leave the ledger. Leave everything. Or I release the real copies to every newspaper in the city."

Meinhardt’s fingers curled around the paper. Crumpled it slowly. "You don’t have the real copies."

Clara smiled. "You’re right. I don’t. But *she* does."

Meinhardt’s eyes flicked to the door behind him.

Viktor stepped forward. "Lotte Weber has your ledger. Every bribe. Every promise. Every name you ever blackmailed."

Clara stood. "Six hours. That’s how long it takes for the first copies to reach the presses. Berlin will know by morning what *real* corruption looks like."

Meinhardt’s breath came faster. His composure frayed like old silk. "You wouldn’t."

Clara walked to the coat rack. Took down Meinhardt’s hat—the one he always wore, the one with the narrow brim, the one that made him look like a man who knew exactly how to lie. She held it out.

Meinhardt didn’t take it.

Clara set it on the table between them. "The train leaves at midnight. Terminal 3. One-way ticket. No return."

Meinhardt’s voice dropped. Low. Dangerous. "You’re making a mistake."

Clara met his gaze. "No. I’m finally making the right one."

Silence settled. The air thickened with old resentments and unspoken threats. Meinhardt’s hands trembled—not with fear, but rage.

Then he reached for his hat.

Clara stepped back. Viktor moved to the door. Opened it.

Meinhardt paused in the doorway. "This isn’t over."

Clara didn’t answer. She just watched him go.

The door clicked shut. Viktor exhaled. "He’ll come back."

Clara walked to the window. Rain streaked the glass, blurring the neon signs outside. "He’ll try."

Viktor joined her at the window. "What now?"

Clara turned. Smiled. "Now we wait for the papers to print."

A knock at the door. Not Meinhardt. Not Viktor. Three sharp raps.

Clara’s smile faded.

Viktor reached for his pistol. Clara put a hand on his arm.

"Expecting someone?" she asked.

Viktor shook his head. "No."

The knock came again. Insistent. Familiar.

Clara crossed the room. Turned the deadbolt. Pulled the door open.

Lotte Weber stood in the hallway, her coat dripping, her violin case clutched tight against her chest. Her eyes were wide, her cheeks flushed from the cold.

Clara stepped aside. "You’re early."

Lotte didn’t move. Didn’t speak. Her gaze flicked past Clara, landing on Viktor.

Viktor lowered his pistol. Slow. Deliberate. "You weren’t supposed to be here."

Lotte’s voice was small. "He knows."

Clara closed the door. Locked it. "Who knows?"

"My uncle." Lotte swallowed. The violin case creaked under her grip. "He knows I have the ledger. He knows I took it. He knows I played it for him."

Viktor cursed under his breath. "How?"

Lotte’s fingers tightened on the case. "He followed me from the Moka. Watched me walk to your door."

Clara looked at Viktor. "You told him?"

Viktor’s face was gray. "No. But he knows you’re involved with her. He knows it all."

Clara turned back to Lotte. "Then he’s coming here."

Lotte nodded. "He’s coming now."
